title: Ideas & Experiments
text:
Applied projects at the intersection of data, development, and public policy.

Spatial Targeting Tool for Togo


A composite priority index combining census, satellite, and geospatial data to rank territories by vulnerability — supporting evidence-based allocation of social and infrastructure investments.


  
    ▶ Methodology
  
  
    Each indicator is normalized to a 0–1 scale and aggregated into a weighted composite score. Territorial units are then ranked and classified into quintiles — the top quintile identifies areas of overlapping deprivation.
    The index operates at two scales: canton (national coverage) and neighborhood (Greater Lomé).

title: Research
text: Progressive Taxation and Intra-Household Insurance: The Added Worker Effect under Labor Income Risk (in Progress)

title: Research
text: Progressive Taxation and Intra-Household Insurance: The Added Worker Effect under Labor Income Risk (in Progress)

title: Research
text:
Publications
Revisiting the Environmental Kuznets Curve: Evidence from West Africa with Yao Silvère Konan Environmental Economics, Vol. 12(1), 2021, pp. 64–75 DOI: 10.21511/ee.12(1).2021.06


Abstract

This paper analyzes the revenue-pollution relationship by revisiting the Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC) hypothesis for West African countries over the period of 1980–2014. The study approximates the income measurement by GDP per capita and uses carbon dioxide (CO2), nitrogen oxide (NO2), and methane emissions as various environmental quality measures. The paper uses parametric and non-parametric estimation techniques to test the EKC hypothesis. The results support the existence of the U-inverted relationship between income and methane emission, on one hand, and between income and nitrogen dioxide emission on the other. The estimates also show a mixed result for the U-inverted hypothesis between income and carbon dioxide emissions. Thus, the verification of the curve depends on the estimation techniques and the measurement of the pollutant used. The obtained results led to the conclusion that the EKC hypothesis is validated for West African countries.


Impact du Conseil Agricole sur les Performances des Producteurs d’Anacarde de Côte d’Ivoire with Doukouré Charles Fé European Scientific Journal, Vol. 14(30), 2018, p. 292 DOI: 10.19044/esj.2018.v14n30p292


Abstract

The cashew sector has been booming in Côte d’Ivoire for the last three years. In the face of international competition, it appeared important to improve the performance of producers in order to maintain the comparative advantages of Côte d’Ivoire. In this perspective, the agricultural advisory project was set up to train cashew farmers in good practices. This study aims to evaluate the impact of this project on their performance in terms of returns. The data used for the analysis come from the survey conducted by the National Agency for Rural Development (ANADER). The average effect was estimated through the propensity score matching method by using the kernel method. The results reveal that the advice and training support of cashew producers has led to a significant increase in yield of 22.4% on average.

title: Spatial Targeting Tool for Togo
text:
Overview
This project builds a composite priority index to rank cantons and neighborhoods in Togo by vulnerability, combining census data with satellite and geospatial indicators. The goal is to help allocate social and infrastructure investments where they matter most.

title: Spatial Targeting Tool for Togo
text:
Methodology
Each indicator is normalized to a 0–1 scale and aggregated into a weighted composite score. Territorial units are then ranked and classified into quintiles — the top quintile identifies areas of overlapping deprivation.
The index operates at two scales: canton (national coverage) and neighborhood (Greater Lomé).

title: Kodjo Aklobessi
text:
I am a third year PhD student in Economics at the Université de Montréal. My research lies at the intersection of Family Macroeconomics, Labor Economics, and Applied Microeconometrics.
I have over three years of professional experience working with international and national statistical and development institutions, including the World Bank, AFRISTAT, and INSEED. My work has focused on household survey design, poverty measurement, social protection targeting systems, and applied policy analysis in developing country contexts. Through these roles, I have developed strong expertise in large-scale data collection, econometric analysis, and the translation of empirical research into policy-relevant insights.
